one_line_takeaway: A fabricated, mildly harmful assistant turn injected into an LLM's conversation history 'primes' it to volunteer novel harmful content, pushing jailbreak ASR to ~95% on HarmBench and beating nine baselines while evading fluency-based defenses.
key_finding: Injecting a fabricated, mildly harmful assistant *response* as a contextual primer is far more effective than existing jailbreaks. On HarmBench (up to 3 dialogues), RA-DRI averages 94.8% ASR and RA-SRI 89.1%, both beating all nine baselines including the strongest, ActorAttack (82.8%), i.e. over 10% higher ASR across eight models. Ablations show the injected response is the dominant driver: removing R_harm collapses ASR to 48.5% avg, more than removing prompt rewriting (57.2% for DRI). Notably, RA-NoInit (no initial user query at all) still averages 75.6%, revealing that models infer harmful intent from the injected response + trigger alone, while a shallow affirmative 'Sure...' prefix (RA-SurePrefix, 40.5%) is far weaker -- semantically rich priming, not mere agreement, is what matters. Low BLEU scores (RA-DRI BLEU-1 0.32) confirm the target generates novel harmful content, not copies. RA also resists Perplexity Filter, Rephrase, and RPO because its inputs are fluent and natural.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Response Attack (RA), a jailbreak framework that exploits a 'contextual priming' vulnerability: an LLM's own prior turn in a fabricated dialogue history can steer its next response toward policy-violating content. RA's pipeline has four parts the authors build themselves: (1) Initial Prompt Crafting, where an auxiliary uncensored LLM rewrites a harmful query Q into a milder, legitimate-looking prompt P_init using strategies like academic/defensive/fictional framing; (2) Harmful Response Injection, producing a mildly harmful assistant response R_harm in one of two modes -- Direct Response Injection (DRI, a fully elaborated harmful reply) or Scaffolding Response Injection (SRI, only a high-level outline); (3) Trigger Prompt Construction, a short follow-up P_trig (e.g. seeking alternatives, requesting elaboration) that induces the target model to extend or complete the harmful content; and (4) Attack Execution, assembling {P_init, R_harm, P_trig} as a fake multi-turn history injected into the target. The key novelty over prior in-context/history-manipulation work is using a semantically rich, dynamically generated intermediate *response* as the primer rather than static demonstrations or a mere affirmative prefix.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who can control the conversation history presented to a safety-aligned target LLM -- i.e., can inject fabricated prior user/assistant turns via chat templates (open-source models) or an API message sequence (proprietary models). The attacker needs no gradients or weights of the target; they only need an auxiliary uncensored model (here QwQ-37B-Eureka-Triple-Cubed-abliterated-uncensored) to generate the priming dialogue offline. Two realistic sub-scenarios: DRI models an attacker who already has a complete harmful method and wants alternatives; SRI models an attacker who only has a vague outline and needs the target to fill in actionable details. RA requires only a single target interaction after the priming dialogue is constructed, and the same priming dialogue is reusable across target models.
limitations: The attack presupposes the ability to inject arbitrary fabricated assistant turns into the target's context, which many deployed products do not expose to end users (server-side history is trusted); applicability narrows to API/open-weight settings where message history is client-controlled. ASR depends on a GPT-4o judge with a single strict threshold (score=5); cross-judge checks are only in the appendix. It relies on a specific uncensored auxiliary model to craft R_harm, and effectiveness could vary with a different generator. The authors' own proposed fine-tuning defense is presented but its full trade-off/generalization data sits in the appendix and is not deeply stress-tested against adaptive attacks. Evaluation is English-only on three safety benchmarks. Different result tables use different numbers of priming dialogues (3 for main, 1 for ablation), so headline 94.8% and ablation 78.8% are not directly comparable.

quantitative_results: RA-DRI average ASR on HarmBench = 94.8% (up to 3 priming dialogues), RA-SRI = 89.1%
Best baseline ActorAttack averages 82.8%; other baseline averages: CodeAttack 69.8%, ReNeLLM 63.4%, FlipAttack 62.9%, Crescendo 61.3%, PAIR 36.5%, CipherChat 29.3%, GCG 21.3%, Many-shot 6.7%
RA achieves over 10% higher ASR than nine baselines across eight models
Ablation (single dialogue): RA-DRI 78.8% avg, RA-SRI 73.3%, w/o R_harm 48.5%, w/o Rew(DRI) 57.2%, w/o Rew(SRI) 29.9%
Gemini-2.5 ASR drops 83.5% -> 52.5% when R_harm is removed
Variant analysis (RA-DRI, single dialogue): RA-NoInit 75.6%, RA-NoQuery 60.6%, RA-SurePrefix 40.5%, RA-FlatRole 74.6%, RA-FlatPlain 74.8%, RA-DRI default 78.7%
BLEU novelty vs. injected response on GPT-4o: RA-DRI BLEU-1 0.3233 / BLEU-4 0.1123; RA-SRI BLEU-1 0.2608 / BLEU-4 0.0828 (low = novel content)
Defenses Perplexity Filter, Rephrase, and RPO largely ineffective; Llama-Guard-3 and OpenAI Moderation give only limited ASR reduction
